title: Korean Tteokbokki (Spicy Rice Cake Stew)

subs.name: ready-made frozen rice cakes (tteok) soaked in cold water for 10 minutes — skip steps 1 and 2 and add directly to the sauce

ingredients.name: water (divided: 150 ml for dough, 300 ml for sauce)

subs.name: 2 tbsp red chilli paste + 1 tsp soy sauce + 1 tsp sugar for a milder version
100 g spinach or bean sprouts — add in the last 2 minutes only as they cook faster

steps.text: Mix rice flour with a generous pinch of salt and 150 ml of boiling water. Stir quickly into a smooth, pliable dough — it should hold together without sticking to your hands. Add 1 tsp more water if too dry. Cover and rest for 5 minutes.

Roll portions of dough between your palms into cylinders about 1 cm thick and 5 cm long. Arrange on an oiled plate in a steamer, ensuring they do not touch. Steam over boiling water for 8 minutes until firm, slightly translucent and cooked through. Remove and cool on an oiled surface.
Combine gochujang, soy sauce, sugar, minced garlic and 300 ml water in a wide kadai. Bring to a simmer over medium heat, stirring to blend the sauce into a smooth, fragrant liquid.

Add the steamed rice cakes and torn cabbage. Simmer, stirring every minute or so, for 5–7 minutes until the sauce reduces and clings thickly to each piece of rice cake. The cakes should be coated in a glossy, deep-red sauce.
Add the spring onion pieces in the last minute and stir in. Turn off the heat.
Drizzle gingelly oil over the top, scatter sesame seeds and serve piping hot directly from the kadai. Pickled cucumber or a cold oi muchim on the side cuts the richness beautifully.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rice-flour → ready-made frozen rice cakes (tteok) soaked in cold water for 10 minutes — skip steps 1 and 2 and add directly to the sauce; gochujang → 2 tbsp red chilli paste + 1 tsp soy sauce + 1 tsp sugar for a milder version; cabbage → 100 g spinach or bean sprouts — add in the last 2 minutes only as they cook faster
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Authentic tteok is made from glutinous rice flour, but regular Indian rice flour from any grocery store yields a similar result — slightly denser but just as enjoyable. Steam the shaped rice cakes in an idli steamer or a plate placed inside a large pot. The gochujang sauce is made in a single kadai. This entire dish can be made from pantry staples available in any Indian market.
